draft.introduction: The Posterior Analytics I.1 opens with Aristotle’s claim that all teaching and learning proceed from pre-existing knowledge. Scientific demonstration (apodeixis) proceeds from premises that are true, primary, and better known than the conclusion.

translation.text: All instruction given or received by way of argument proceeds from pre-existing knowledge. This becomes evident if we survey all the species of such instruction: the mathematical sciences and every other art are acquired in this way. So too with dialectical arguments, whether syllogistic or inductive; both proceed from pre-existing knowledge.
translation.original_text: πᾶσα διδασκαλία καὶ πᾶσα μάθησις διανοητικὴ ἐκ προϋπαρχούσης γίνεται γνώσεως

translator_notes.note: The requirement that all learning begins from prior knowledge raises the same problem as Meno’s paradox. Aristotle’s solution—that we can know universals potentially through experience while learning them actually through demonstration—contrasts with Plato’s doctrine of recollection.
